title: The Nutrient Run
desc: Swamp mud feeds the fungus. Hauling it back is the hard part.

steps.text:
Gloomuck Swamp is disgusting. This is not an opinion — it's a documented fact, recorded in Danian agricultural manuals under the heading "Nutrient Source: High-Value, Low-Appeal."

The swamp produces the richest organic substrate in the region — a black, reeking mud packed with decomposed plant matter, mineral deposits, and microorganisms that fungal crops devour. A single cart of Gloomuck mud can fertilize an entire garden section for a season.

The hauling crew stands at the swamp's edge, loading equipment ready. The crew chief distributes cloth masks — not for protection, but for the smell.

"Standard run. Fill eight barrels. Don't step in anything that bubbles. Don't drink anything. Don't touch the yellow patches — that's sulfur deposit and it'll burn through chitin."

She wades in. The mud reaches her knees immediately.

options.label: Wade in and fill barrels — the direct approach
Work from the bank — use the extension poles

steps.text:
The mud is warm. Not comfortably warm — biologically warm. The decomposition process generates heat, and standing in Gloomuck mud is like standing in a slow, organic furnace. Your feet sink with each step. The smell penetrates the mask.

You fill barrels by scooping — plunging a bucket deep and pulling up the thick, black substrate. Each bucket load is heavy, clinging, reluctant to leave the swamp. You pour it into barrels staged at the water's edge.

Four barrels in, you find a pocket of the good stuff — a deep layer where the mud is almost pure compost, dark as ink and rich with nutrients. The garden overseer will be pleased. You fill the remaining barrels from this pocket.

The haul back to Mount Pillar is uphill. Eight full barrels on two carts, pushed by a crew that smells worse than the cargo. Other Danians give you a wide berth in the tunnels. The nutrient runners are respected for their work and avoided for their aroma.

The garden overseer tests the mud and grins. "Prime substrate. This pocket is high-nitrogen — the fungus will bloom within days." She marks the pocket's location on the swamp map. "Good find. We'll send a crew back before the season turns."

You spend the evening scrubbing. The smell takes three washes to fade. The work, however, matters — the fungus gardens that feed the hive depend on people willing to wade into Gloomuck and come back carrying the worst-smelling cargo in Perim.

options.label: Scrub off the swamp and take the compliment

steps.text:
The extension poles are ten-foot handles with collection buckets on the end — designed for gathering substrate without wading in. Slower than direct collection, but you stay dry, clean, and unbitten by whatever lives in the mud.

You plant yourself on a stable section of bank and swing the pole out. The bucket hits the mud surface and sinks. You pull — heavy, straining, the mud reluctant to release its grip. The bucket comes up half-full, dripping black.

It takes twice as many pulls to fill each barrel. The other crew members wade in for speed, but you keep working from the bank, finding a rhythm — swing, sink, pull, pour. Your arms burn after an hour.

The advantage of bank work becomes clear when a crew member in the swamp stumbles into a sulfur pocket. Yellow foam erupts around their legs. They scramble out, cursing, the chitin on their shins already discolored. Not burned through — the exposure was brief — but a reminder of why the crew chief warned about yellow patches.

You help treat the sulfur marks with neutralizing paste from the first-aid kit. The affected crew member sits out the rest of the run. Your barrels, filled from the bank, are sulfur-free.

The crew chief notes your approach. "Slower but cleaner. And nobody got burned." She adjusts the next run's plan. "More extension poles. Fewer waders. Safety over speed."

Your arms will ache for days, but your chitin is intact.

options.label: Accept the muscle ache as a fair trade
